Midterm Practical - Spring 2026 - KEY

Please see below for answers to all parts of the Midterm. Note, I provide you one way to do the code - there are others. You must demonstrate you have the correct answer for full credit.

Answers

Section 1

library(tidyverse)
svad <- read.csv("data/svad_lb.csv")

Question 1

# Inspect the dataset

glimpse(svad)
Rows: 1,317
Columns: 21
$ SVAD_identifier <chr> "US_0186", "US_0006", "US_0837", "US_0915", "US_0617",…
$ SEX             <chr> "F", "F", "M", "M", "M", "F", "M", "M", "F", "F", "M",…
$ agey            <dbl> 1.17260274, 4.75616438, 4.07397260, 1.08767123, 0.8301…
$ FDL_L           <dbl> 141.79, 257.30, 211.32, 144.60, 124.93, NA, NA, 228.58…
$ FMSB_L          <dbl> 12.60, 15.85, 13.37, 12.37, 11.35, NA, NA, 17.54, NA, …
$ FDB_L           <dbl> 38.46, 52.85, 43.20, 39.89, 38.77, 67.15, 73.32, 52.33…
$ TDL_L           <dbl> 114.88, 210.03, 171.10, 120.40, 101.25, NA, NA, 190.47…
$ TPB_L           <dbl> 29.93, 43.73, 35.83, 31.06, 29.75, NA, 59.61, 42.13, 4…
$ TMSB_L          <dbl> 10.43, 14.95, 12.77, 10.80, 9.62, NA, NA, 12.87, 14.70…
$ TDB_L           <dbl> 19.46, 30.35, 23.80, 19.89, 19.79, 53.77, 45.27, 27.03…

$ HDL_L           <dbl> 105.94, 188.89, 150.20, 111.71, 100.81, NA, NA, 169.49…
$ HPB_L           <dbl> 21.71, 27.92, 23.38, 23.13, 23.65, 41.98, 44.32, 26.58…
$ HMSB_L          <dbl> 12.27, 15.20, 11.23, 12.20, 12.35, NA, NA, 13.77, NA, …
$ HDB_L           <dbl> 27.41, 38.28, 27.84, 28.34, 27.75, 46.30, 56.92, 34.94…
$ RDL_L           <dbl> 83.86, 143.38, 111.39, 88.97, 77.32, NA, NA, 129.84, 1…
$ RPB_L           <dbl> 11.26, 12.17, 9.70, 11.25, 10.37, NA, 20.49, 11.53, 12…
$ RMSB_L          <dbl> 8.65, 10.36, 7.77, 8.94, 7.46, NA, NA, 9.22, 9.51, 7.9…
$ RDB_L           <dbl> 15.70, NA, 14.40, 15.55, 15.71, 23.86, 30.56, 17.13, 1…
$ UDL_L           <dbl> 95.76, 158.56, 123.06, 101.44, 86.30, NA, NA, 143.00, …
$ UMSB_L          <dbl> 8.11, 9.74, 7.68, 8.42, 8.74, NA, NA, 9.16, 10.09, 7.5…
# Replace all -1 values with NA
svad_clean <- svad %>%
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ na_if(.x, -1)))

# Check
svad_clean %>%
  summarise(across(where(is.numeric), ~ any(.x == -1, na.rm = TRUE)))
   agey FDL_L FMSB_L FDB_L TDL_L TPB_L TMSB_L TDB_L FBDL_L HDL_L HPB_L HMSB_L
1 FALSE FALSE  FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE  FALSE FALSE  FALSE FALSE FALSE  FALSE
  HDB_L RDL_L RPB_L RMSB_L RDB_L UDL_L UMSB_L
1 FALSE FALSE FALSE  FALSE FALSE FALSE  FALSE
# Filter for female individuals and count missing values in measurement columns
# Adjust 'sex' and female indicator based on actual column name/coding in dataset
female_missing <- svad_clean %>%
  filter(SEX == "F") %>%  
  select(where(is.numeric)) %>%
  summarise(across(everything(), ~ sum(is.na(.x)))) %>%
  pivot_longer(everything(),
               names_to  = "column",
               values_to = "missing_count")
female_missing

Question 2

crural <- svad_clean %>%
  filter(agey >= 0 & agey <= 5,
         FDL_L >= 90) %>%
  select(contains(c("FDL", "TDL", "FBDL", "HDL", "RDL", "UDL"))) %>%
  mutate(crural = TDL_L / FDL_L)

head(crural, 3)
   FDL_L  TDL_L FBDL_L  HDL_L  RDL_L  UDL_L    crural
1 141.79 114.88 110.76 105.94  83.86  95.76 0.8102123
2 257.30 210.03 212.17 188.89 143.38 158.56 0.8162845
3 211.32 171.10 168.31 150.20 111.39 123.06 0.8096725

Question 3

group1 <- svad_clean %>% filter(SEX != "NA") %>%
  group_by(SEX) %>%
  summarise(
    across(
      contains(c("FDL", "TDL", "FBDL", "HDL", "RDL", "UDL")),
      list(
        n   = ~ sum(!is.na(.x)),
        mean = ~ mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE),
        sd   = ~ sd(.x, na.rm = TRUE)
      )
    )
  ) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols      = -SEX,
    names_to  = c("measurement", "stat"),
    names_sep = "_(?=[^_]+$)",  # splits on the LAST underscore
    values_to = "value"
  )

head(group1)
# A tibble: 6 × 4
  SEX   measurement stat  value
  <chr> <chr>       <chr> <dbl>
1 F     FDL_L       n     245  
2 F     FDL_L       mean  168. 
3 F     FDL_L       sd     90.9
4 F     TDL_L       n     253  
5 F     TDL_L       mean  142. 
6 F     TDL_L       sd     75.7

Question 4

group2 <- svad_clean %>%
  mutate(age_int = as.integer(agey)) %>%
  group_by(age_int) %>%
  summarise(
    across(
      contains(c("FMSB", "FDB", "TPB", "TMSB", "TDB")),
      list(
        n    = ~ sum(!is.na(.x)),
        mean = ~ mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE),
        sd   = ~ sd(.x, na.rm = TRUE)
      )
    )
  ) %>%  pivot_longer(
    cols      = -age_int,
    names_to  = c("measurement", "stat"),
    names_sep = "_(?=[^_]+$)",  # splits on the LAST underscore
    values_to = "value"
  )

head(group2)
# A tibble: 6 × 4
  age_int measurement stat   value
    <int> <chr>       <chr>  <dbl>
1       0 FMSB_L      n     245   
2       0 FMSB_L      mean    9.27
3       0 FMSB_L      sd      1.64
4       0 FDB_L       n     246   
5       0 FDB_L       mean   26.5 
6       0 FDB_L       sd      5.34

Question 5

svad_clean <- svad_clean %>%
  rename_with(~ str_remove(.x, "_L$") %>% str_to_lower(),
              ends_with("_L"))

# Print column names to verify
names(svad_clean)
 [1] "SVAD_identifier" "SEX"             "agey"            "fdl"            
 [5] "fmsb"            "fdb"             "tdl"             "tpb"

Question 6

palm_clean <- palm %>% na.omit()

ggplot(palm_clean, aes(x = stem_solitary, y = max_stem_dia_cm, fill = stem_solitary)) +
  geom_violin(alpha = 0.4) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("non-solitary" = "steelblue", "solitary" = "tomato", "both" = "goldenrod")) +
  labs(
    title    = "Maximum Stem Diameter by Stem Growth Form",
    subtitle = "Comparison of solitary and clustered palm species",
    x        = "Stem Growth Form",
    y        = "Maximum Stem Diameter (cm)",
    fill     = "Growth Form"
  ) +
  theme_classic(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    plot.title      = element_text(face = "bold", hjust = 0.5),
    plot.subtitle   = element_text(hjust = 0.5, color = "grey40"),
    legend.position = "none",
    axis.line       = element_line(color = "black"),
    axis.text       = element_text(color = "black")
  )

Question 7

There is a linear relationship between stem height and diameter with a taller stem generally associated with a larger diameter.

palm_clean %>% ggplot(aes(max_stem_height_m, max_stem_dia_cm, colour = stem_solitary)) + geom_point() + geom_smooth(se=F, method = "lm") + theme_minimal()
`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

Question 8

Could be anything within reason.

palm_clean %>% ggplot(aes(average_fruit_length_cm, average_fruit_width_cm, colour = fruit_size_categorical)) + geom_point() + geom_smooth(se=F, method = "lm") + theme_minimal()
`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

Section 3

Question 9

Normality

Data are non-normal.

qqnorm(diwali$Amount, frame = F)
qqline(diwali$Amount, col = "steelblue", lwd = 3)
ggplot(diwali, aes(x = Amount)) + theme_bw() + geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)), color = "black", fill = "white") + geom_density(fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.4) + facet_grid(cols = vars(Zone))
`stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value `binwidth`.
Warning: Removed 12 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
(`stat_bin()`).
Warning: Removed 12 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
(`stat_density()`).

Homoscedasticity

Variance is NOT homoskedastic.

car::leveneTest(Amount ~ Zone, data = diwali)
Warning in leveneTest.default(y = y, group = group, ...): group coerced to
factor.
Levene's Test for Homogeneity of Variance (center = median)
         Df F value    Pr(>F)    
group     4  60.805 < 2.2e-16 ***
      11234                      
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Question 10

While this may show “outliers”, I see no reason to remove given they could be realistic.

diwali %>% ggplot(aes(x = Zone, y = Amount)) + theme_bw() + geom_boxplot(outlier.colour = "red", outlier.size = 3)
Warning: Removed 12 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
(`stat_boxplot()`).

Question 11

The data are non-normal with more than 2 groups. Therefore, I use the kruskal wallis test.

There are statistically significant differences in spending by zone across most regional comparisons.

kruskal.test(Amount ~ Zone, data = diwali)

    Kruskal-Wallis rank sum test

data:  Amount by Zone
Kruskal-Wallis chi-squared = 75.83, df = 4, p-value = 1.33e-15
library(dunn.test)

print(dunn.test(diwali$Amount, diwali$Zone, method = "holm"))
  Kruskal-Wallis rank sum test

data: x and group
Kruskal-Wallis chi-squared = 75.8303, df = 4, p-value = 0

         │

Question 12

The data are non-normal. The variance is homoscedastic. No outliers were removed. A man-whitney test is run because of the binary category.

There are not statistically significant differences by marital status.

ggplot(diwali, aes(x = Amount)) + theme_bw() + geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)), color = "black", fill = "white") + geom_density(fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.4) + facet_grid(cols = vars(Marital_Status))
`stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value `binwidth`.
Warning: Removed 12 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
(`stat_bin()`).
Warning: Removed 12 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
(`stat_density()`).
car::leveneTest(Amount ~ as.factor(Marital_Status), data = diwali)
Levene's Test for Homogeneity of Variance (center = median)
         Df F value Pr(>F)
group     1  0.5159 0.4726
      11237               
diwali %>% ggplot(aes(x = as.factor(Marital_Status), y = Amount)) + theme_bw() + geom_boxplot(outlier.colour = "red", outlier.size = 3)
Warning: Removed 12 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
(`stat_boxplot()`).
wilcox.test(Amount ~ Marital_Status, data = diwali)

    Wilcoxon rank sum test with continuity correction

data:  Amount by Marital_Status
W = 15706055, p-value = 0.0592
alternative hypothesis: true location shift is not equal to 0

Section 4

penguin <- penguins

Question 13

min_max <- function(body_mass) {
  minimum <- sort(body_mass, na.last = NA)[1]
  maximum <- sort(body_mass, na.last = NA)[length(sort(body_mass, na.last = NA))]
  
  return(c(minimum = minimum, maximum = maximum))
}

# Example usage
min_max(penguin$body_mass)
minimum maximum 
   2700    6300 
# Check
min(penguin$body_mass, na.rm = T)
[1] 2700
max(penguin$body_mass, na.rm = T)
[1] 6300

Question 14

# Define the columns to iterate over
cols <- c("bill_len", "bill_dep", "flipper_len", "body_mass")

# Empty data frame to store results
results <- data.frame(
  variable = character(),
  minimum  = numeric(),
  maximum  = numeric()
)

# For loop
for (col in cols) {
  vals    <- min_max(penguins[[col]])
  results <- rbind(results, data.frame(
    variable = col,
    minimum  = vals["minimum"],
    maximum  = vals["maximum"],
    row.names = NULL
  ))
}

print(results)
     variable minimum maximum
1    bill_len    32.1    59.6
2    bill_dep    13.1    21.5
3 flipper_len   172.0   231.0
4   body_mass  2700.0  6300.0
